nav.confirm_leave: Your progress in this chapter will be saved.

dev.untestedHint: Not yet tested. After testing, set 'tested: false' → 'tested: true' in src/components/story/storyChapters.config.js.

chapter1.introNarrator: The Stellar blockchain – a digital network moving value around the globe. And tumbling right into it falls a small star...
chapter1.introDialog1: Hi! I'm Lumio and I just landed on the Stellar blockchain! 🌟
chapter1.introDialog2: But I don't have an account here yet... can you help me create one?
chapter1.keypairNarrator: Every account on Stellar consists of two keys: the [[Public Key|publicKey]] (your address) and the [[Secret Key|privateKey]] (your password). Only someone who knows the Secret Key can send money. The Public Key works similarly to an IBAN or account number – you share it with others so they can send you payments.
chapter1.choicePublicKeyLabel: I share the Public Key with others so they can send me XLM.
chapter1.choicePublicKeyHint: Exactly! The [[Public Key|publicKey]] is like a bank account number – you can share it publicly. ✓
chapter1.choiceSecretShareLabel: I give my Secret Key to someone I trust.
chapter1.choiceSecretShareHint: Careful! NOBODY should know the [[Secret Key|privateKey]] – not even Stellar or Lumio. It gives full access to your account.
chapter1.choiceSameLabel: Both keys are actually the same thing.
chapter1.choiceSameHint: That's not correct. The Public and Secret Key form a pair, but have completely different roles.
chapter1.createDialog: Great! Now let's create our shared test account. Press the button – I'll keep watch!
chapter1.createActionDesc: Creates a random Public Key and Secret Key for the Stellar Testnet. For practice purposes only!
chapter1.warningDialog1: Wow, we now have a keypair! Never show your Secret Key to anyone!
chapter1.warningDialog2: On the real network I'd recommend writing it on paper and keeping it somewhere safe.
chapter1.activateNarrator: A new Stellar account must first be activated – it needs at least 1 XLM as a reserve. On the Testnet, the '[[Friendbot|friendbot]]' gives us free test XLM.
chapter1.friendbotDesc: Sends a request to the Stellar Friendbot, which activates our test account with 10,000 test XLM.
chapter1.friendbotError: No keypair found – please generate one first.
chapter1.outroDialog1: We have a real account on the Stellar Testnet! I can already see the XLM in the blockchain...
chapter1.outroDialog2: You learned that quickly! In the next chapter we'll send XLM together.
chapter1.summaryLearning1: Every Stellar account consists of a Public Key (address) and Secret Key (password)
chapter1.summaryLearning2: The Secret Key must never be shared
chapter1.summaryLearning3: New accounts need at least 1 XLM minimum balance (base reserve)
chapter1.summaryLearning4: The Friendbot activates Testnet accounts for free
chapter1.keypair_warning: It cannot be recovered. Anyone who knows it has full access to your account.
chapter1.keypair_confirm: I understand that my Secret Key cannot be recovered.
chapter1.keypair_secret_label: Your Secret Key – KEEP IT SECRET!
chapter1.explorer_desc: In Stellar Explorer you can publicly view your account and all transactions – like a bank statement, but visible to everyone.
chapter1.explorer_hint: Copy the address and open it manually in your browser.
chapter1.explorer_confirm_text: You will be redirected to {{explorer}}. A new tab will open.
chapter1.summaryLearning4a: On the Testnet, Friendbot activates accounts for free with test XLM
chapter1.summaryLearning4b: On the real network (Mainnet), another account must send at least 1 XLM to activate your account – Friendbot doesn't exist there!
chapter1.title: Create Account
chapter1.explorer_after_fund: After account activation you can view it in the Explorer
chapter1.q1_question: What do you do with your [[Public Key|publicKey]]?
chapter1.subtitle: Wallet & Keypair

chapter2.title: Lumio's First Transaction
chapter2.narrator_intro: Lumio now has an account with test XLM. But having money isn't enough – you also need to be able to send it. How does that work on Stellar?
chapter2.lumio_1a: I want to send 10 XLM to my friend Cosmo! But how does that work?
chapter2.lumio_1b: I hear there's something called a transaction... what exactly is that?
chapter2.narrator_tx: A [[transaction|transaction]] on Stellar is an instruction to the network: send 10 XLM from account A to account B. The network checks the balance, deducts a small [[base fee|base_fee]], and writes everything to the [[ledger|ledger]] – the network's general ledger. Like entries in a bookkeeping record, all entries are stored permanently and linked like chain links: no entry can be removed without it being immediately noticed.
chapter2.choice1_a: It pays the network nodes and prevents spam transactions.
chapter2.choice1_b: It goes directly to the Stellar Development Foundation as profit.
chapter2.choice1_c: It's optional – you can leave it out.
chapter2.choice1_a_hint: Exactly! The [[base fee|base_fee]] is very small (0.00001 XLM) but important for network security.
chapter2.choice1_b_hint: Wrong – the fees are burned, not collected.
chapter2.choice1_c_hint: Wrong – without a fee the transaction will be rejected.
chapter2.lumio_2: And what is this Memo field I see in some wallets?
chapter2.narrator_memo: The [[Memo|memo]] field works like the message field in a bank transfer – you can use it to describe or identify a payment (maximum 28 characters). Important: crypto exchanges require the Memo field when depositing to exchange wallets. Without the correct Memo your funds can be lost!
chapter2.choice2_a: Always – without a Memo the transaction will be rejected.
chapter2.choice2_b: When I want to clearly identify my payment and make clear what purpose it was made for.
chapter2.choice2_c: Never – the Memo field is outdated and no longer used.
chapter2.choice2_a_hint: Not always – between regular wallets the Memo is optional.
chapter2.choice2_b_hint: Correct! At exchanges the [[Memo|memo]] identifies your account.
chapter2.choice2_c_hint: Wrong – Memo is active and essential for exchange deposits.
chapter2.lumio_3: Ok, I think I understand! Let's try a real transaction!
chapter2.action_desc: Sends 10 test XLM to Cosmo's Testnet account with Memo 'Hallo Cosmo'. Afterwards you'll see the transaction hash on the blockchain.
chapter2.error_no_keypair: No keypair found – please complete Chapter 1 first.
chapter2.lumio_4a: I see it! The transaction is on the blockchain! The [[transaction hash|transaction_hash]] is like a receipt number – anyone can look up the transaction with it.
chapter2.lumio_4b: In the Stellar Explorer you can publicly view any transaction using the hash.
chapter2.narrator_hash: The [[transaction hash|transaction_hash]] is a unique ID for every transaction. It proves beyond doubt that the transaction took place.
chapter2.learning_1: A transaction sends XLM from one account to another
chapter2.learning_2: The base fee (0.00001 XLM) pays the network and prevents spam
chapter2.learning_3: The Memo field helps to clearly identify payments and state their purpose. For exchange deposits it is essential – without the correct memo, your funds can be lost. Some addresses even require the memo technically.
chapter2.learning_4: Every transaction has a unique hash as proof
chapter2.q1_question: What is the purpose of the [[Base Fee|base_fee]] in a Stellar transaction?
chapter2.q2_question: When do you need the [[Memo|memo]] field?
chapter2.subtitle: Transactions & Memo

ui.glossary_hint: You can learn more about this in the glossary.

placeholder.wip_text: This chapter is still being built. Lumio is working hard on it – check back soon!
placeholder.wip_hint: Chapter {{next}} is also still in progress – but you can already take a look.

chapter3.title: The Merchant
chapter3.narrator_intro: A marketplace on the Stellar network. Lumio discovers a colourful fruit stall – and the air carries the sweet scent of fresh strawberries...
chapter3.marco_1a: 🍓 Marco: Welcome! I'm Marco, the fruit merchant. Every one of my BERRY tokens represents 1 kg of fresh strawberries – right here on the Stellar blockchain!
chapter3.marco_1b: 🍓 Marco: I'd love to send you 10 BERRY as a gift! But first we need to solve a small problem...
chapter3.lumio_1: A problem? What do you mean, Marco?
chapter3.lumio_2: What exactly is an Asset on Stellar? And what makes someone an Anchor?
chapter3.narrator_asset: On Stellar, anyone can issue their own token – we call that an '[[Asset|asset]]'. An [[Anchor|anchor]] is a real organisation that represents real-world value (like strawberries or money) through Stellar tokens.
chapter3.marco_asset_1: 🍓 Marco: Exactly! I am an Anchor – my BERRY tokens are backed by real strawberries in my warehouse. Every token can be redeemed for 100 g of strawberries.
chapter3.marco_asset_3: 🍓 Marco: I am the bridge between the real world and the blockchain. Whoever gives me real money gets BERRY tokens on Stellar. And whoever gives back BERRY tokens gets real money back.
chapter3.marco_asset_2: 🍓 Marco: For your account to receive BERRY tokens, you must first enable BERRY for your account. This is called a Trustline on Stellar.
chapter3.narrator_trustline_intro: A [[Trust Line|trustline]] is a permission to receive a specific token. Without one, Stellar rejects incoming tokens – protecting you from unwanted or dangerous tokens.
chapter3.narrator_tl_why: Why does the issuer address matter? Anyone could create a token called 'BERRY'. Only Code + Issuer Address together make a token uniquely identifiable.
chapter3.tl_methods_title: 4 ways to add a Trust Line
chapter3.tl_method1: Manually: enter the Asset Code and Issuer Address directly
chapter3.tl_method2: QR Code / Deep Link: form is auto-filled – convenient, but beware of fake codes
chapter3.tl_method3: TOML file (SEP-1): anchor publishes its assets on its official domain
chapter3.tl_method4: Pick from the search list: search for an asset code or name in your wallet – verified tokens appear directly in the search results
chapter3.marco_danger: 🍓 Marco: I've heard something alarming: someone has been handing out fake BERRY QR codes around town! Please be careful.
chapter3.narrator_danger_qr: A fake QR code can point to a wrong issuer address. The token might still be called 'BERRY' – but it's worthless, because it comes from the wrong issuer.
chapter3.narrator_danger_toml: Phishing domains are dangerous too: 'marco-berry.com' and 'marcoberry.com' look almost identical. Always check the exact spelling of the domain.
chapter3.narrator_verify:
How to identify the real issuer:
• Open stellar.expert and look up the issuer address – a verified issuer has a visible 'Home Domain' field
• Via the Home Domain you can reach the anchor's stellar.toml file – it lists all official assets with their issuer addresses
• stellar.expert displays a blue 'Verified Asset' badge for tokens whose stellar.toml is correctly configured
• Always compare: issuer address in the app ↔ address in the anchor's stellar.toml
chapter3.q_qr: Lumio receives two QR codes for BERRY tokens. Which one is real?
chapter3.choice_real_label: QR Code A: Issuer GBERRY...A7F2 (verified on stellar.expert – Marco's official address)
chapter3.choice_real_hint: Correct! This code comes from Marco's real issuer account, verified on stellar.expert. Always compare with the official website.
chapter3.choice_fake_label: QR Code B: Issuer GBERRY...B8G3 (unknown address, no verification found)
chapter3.choice_fake_hint: Watch out! This issuer address is unknown. The tokens would be worthless. Always verify the issuer first.
chapter3.marco_before_tl: 🍓 Marco: Now you know how to tell real trust lines from fake ones. Let's set up the real trust line – then I can send you your 10 BERRY!
chapter3.action_tl_desc: Sets up a Trust Line for BERRY tokens from Marco's Testnet issuer account. Enables receiving BERRY tokens.
chapter3.error_no_keypair: No keypair found – please complete Chapter 1 first.
chapter3.q1_question: Which 3 pieces of information uniquely define a [[Trust Line|trustline]]?
chapter3.q1_a: Asset Code + Issuer Address + holding account
chapter3.q1_a_hint: Correct! Asset Code, Issuer Address, and the account holding the trust line make it unique. The Limit is just an optional property.
chapter3.q1_b_hint: No – those are keypair components, not trust line elements.
chapter3.q1_c_hint: No – those are transaction fields, not trust line components.
chapter3.q2_question: Why can't a BERRY token from Issuer A be confused with a BERRY token from Issuer B?
chapter3.q2_a: Because only one single 'BERRY' token can exist on Stellar
chapter3.q2_a_hint: Wrong – anyone can create a token called BERRY. The issuer address makes it unique.
chapter3.q2_b: Because the issuer address uniquely identifies the token – same name, different issuers = different tokens
chapter3.q2_b_hint: Correct! Only the combination of Asset Code + Issuer Address creates a unique asset on Stellar.
chapter3.q2_c: Because different tokens must always have different names
chapter3.q2_c_hint: Wrong – tokens can share the same name. The issuer address distinguishes them.
chapter3.q3_question: You receive a QR code in a chat message asking you to add a Trust Line. What do you do?
chapter3.q3_a: I scan the code right away – it's just a trust line, not real money
chapter3.q3_a_hint: Watch out! Trust lines can be risky too – the token could be worthless or a phishing trap. Always verify first.
chapter3.q3_b: I verify the issuer on stellar.expert and the anchor's official website first
chapter3.q3_b_hint: Exactly right! Never trust a QR code blindly – always verify the issuer address and the domain.
chapter3.q3_c: I ask a friend whether they know the QR code
chapter3.q3_c_hint: Better than trusting blindly – but the most reliable method is direct verification on stellar.expert.
chapter3.marco_send: 🍓 Marco: Now that you've set up the trust line, I can finally send you the 10 BERRY tokens! That's exactly what the trust line was needed for.
chapter3.lumio_receive: Ah, I understand! Without the trust line, Stellar would have simply rejected the tokens – no matter who sent them?
chapter3.marco_confirm: 🍓 Marco: Exactly! The network would have responded: 'This account does not accept BERRY.' The trust line is your explicit permission.
chapter3.action_receive_desc: Marco sends 10 BERRY tokens to your account. This works only because you set up the trust line in the previous scene.
chapter3.learning_1: An Asset is any token issued on Stellar – from XLM to BERRY
chapter3.learning_2: An Anchor connects real-world value (e.g. strawberries, money) with Stellar tokens
chapter3.learning_3: A Trust Line consists of: Asset Code + Issuer Address + Limit
chapter3.learning_4: Only Asset Code + Issuer Address together uniquely identify a token
chapter3.learning_5: Fake QR codes & phishing domains: always verify the issuer on stellar.expert (Home Domain, Verified Asset badge) and the official anchor website
chapter3.subtitle: Assets & Trust Lines

chapter4.title: The Villain
chapter4.section_emergency: Step 6 – What To Do in an Emergency
chapter4.intro_narrator1: Lumio just received their first BERRY token and is exploring the Stellar world. Then a message arrives from a stranger...
chapter4.intro_narrator2: Sometimes the most dangerous enemies aren't hackers with code – they're people who exploit your trust.
chapter4.scam_msg1: Hello! I'm from official Stellar Support. We've detected unusual activity on your account. 🚨
chapter4.scam_msg2: To protect your account, we need to verify your identity. Please send us your Secret Key.
chapter4.scam_msg3: This is completely safe and confidential. Send us your Secret Key within 3 minutes – for one-time verification only.
chapter4.social_eng_desc: Attackers use psychology instead of technology. They play with emotions like fear, greed, or trust to manipulate you.
chapter4.tactic1_text: "I'm from official Support" – real services NEVER ask for your Secret Key.
chapter4.tactic2_text: "Only 3 minutes!" – Artificial time pressure prevents you from thinking clearly.
chapter4.tactic3_text: "Double XLM back!" – If it sounds too good to be true, it usually is.
chapter4.tactic4_text: Mixing small truths to then place a big lie.
chapter4.choice1_question: CryptoHelper99 is asking for your Secret Key. What do you do?
chapter4.choice1_yes: I'll send the Secret Key – Support needs it after all.
chapter4.choice1_yes_hint: Never! Real support NEVER asks for the Secret Key.
chapter4.choice1_no: I ignore the message – no legitimate support asks for that.
chapter4.choice1_no_hint: Correct! No Stellar service or support will ever ask for your Secret Key.
chapter4.choice1_ask: I ask whether this is really necessary.
chapter4.choice1_ask_hint: Better than sharing, but the right step is to simply ignore and report.
chapter4.choice1_hint2: Remember: whoever has your Secret Key has EVERYTHING. There is never a reason to share it.
chapter4.drain_text: CryptoHelper99 used your Secret Key to transfer all XLM and tokens in seconds. Your account is empty.
chapter4.after_choice1_1: Well done! The Secret Key is like a master key to your house. Give it away and anyone can get in.
chapter4.after_choice1_2: Real Stellar support, wallets, or apps will NEVER ask for your Secret Key. That's an absolute red flag!
chapter4.choice2_question: CryptoHelper99 now sends you a link: 'stellar-support-verify.net'. What do you do?
chapter4.choice2_click: I click the link – I want to see what's behind it.
chapter4.choice2_click_hint: Phishing sites often look deceptively real and steal your data.
chapter4.choice2_ignore: I ignore the link and report the account as a scam.
chapter4.choice2_ignore_hint: Perfect! Always ignore and report unknown links from strangers.
chapter4.choice2_reply: I reply and ask why the link is needed.
chapter4.choice2_reply_hint: Any interaction tells the scammer your account is active – better to ignore completely.
chapter4.choice2_hint2: Phishing URLs look legitimate, but the domain is always wrong. Stellar only uses stellar.org.
chapter4.mini_sim_msg1: Congratulations! You've been selected for an exclusive XLM airdrop! 🎉
chapter4.mini_sim_msg2: To claim your 500 XLM, just enter your Secret Key to verify your wallet.
chapter4.mini_sim_msg3: Offer valid for 10 minutes only! Hurry up!
chapter4.mini_sim_opt_report: I report the message as fraud.
chapter4.mini_sim_scam_desc: A real airdrop never needs your Secret Key. With this key, the attacker would have immediate full access to your account.
chapter4.mini_sim_safe_desc: No legitimate service needs your Secret Key. Airdrops work via your Public Key – not the Secret Key.
chapter4.emergency_desc: If you think your Secret Key was stolen or shared, act immediately:
chapter4.emergency1: Act immediately: Every minute counts – open your wallet app.
chapter4.emergency2: Transfer all funds: Send XLM and all tokens immediately to a new, secure address.
chapter4.emergency3: Remove trusted addresses: Review all set trust lines.
chapter4.emergency4: Create a new keypair: Generate a completely new keypair and never use the old one again.
chapter4.emergency5: Report it: Inform the community and report scam addresses on relevant platforms.
chapter4.quiz1_q: What does a legitimate support agent NEVER do?
chapter4.quiz1_a1_hint: That alone is no problem – many services have live chat.
chapter4.quiz1_a2_hint: Correct! That's the ultimate red flag. Real support never needs your Secret Key.
chapter4.quiz1_a3_hint: Legitimate help can explain things – that's fine.
chapter4.quiz2_q: You receive a message: 'Send 1 XLM and get 10 back'. What is this?
chapter4.quiz2_a1: A legitimate offer from a verified provider
chapter4.quiz2_a1_hint: Such offers are never legitimate, no matter who makes them.
chapter4.quiz2_a2_hint: No – this sounds like a real scam attempt.
chapter4.quiz2_a3: A classic scam – too good to be true
chapter4.quiz2_a3_hint: Exactly! This pattern is one of the oldest crypto scams and unfortunately still works.
chapter4.quiz3_q: Your Secret Key may have been stolen. What is the FIRST step?
chapter4.quiz3_a1: Immediately transfer all funds to a new account
chapter4.quiz3_a1_hint: Correct! Every second counts – save what can be saved first.
chapter4.quiz3_a2: Ask the attacker to return the funds
chapter4.quiz3_a2_hint: That never works – blockchain transactions are irreversible.
chapter4.quiz3_a3: Call Stellar Support to freeze the account
chapter4.quiz3_a3_hint: Blockchain accounts cannot be frozen – that's a myth.
chapter4.learning1: Social engineering uses psychology, not technology – trust, fear, time pressure
chapter4.learning2: No service, support, or app ever asks for your Secret Key
chapter4.learning3: Ignore and report unknown links – phishing often looks deceptively real
chapter4.learning4: Was your Secret Key compromised? Transfer all funds immediately!
chapter4.learning5: Offers that seem too good (10x back, free airdrop) are always scams
chapter4.subtitle: Scams & Social Engineering

chapter5.title: Lumio's Mission
chapter5.intro_narrator1: Congratulations – you've mastered all four chapters and now know how Stellar accounts, payments, tokens, and security work.
chapter5.intro_narrator2: Lumio has one final mission for you...
chapter5.lumio_intro1: You've learned so much! Now comes the final test: Can you help a real person understand Stellar?
chapter5.lumio_intro2: Your friend Lena has heard about Stellar and wants to receive her first payment. Are you in?
chapter5.lena_greeting: Hey! I heard you know a lot about Stellar?
chapter5.lena_request: Can you send me 1 XLM? I want to see how it works!
chapter5.lesson1_text: Public Key = account number, Secret Key = PIN. Your key pair protects all your funds.
chapter5.lesson2_text: Payments on Stellar are fast (3–5 sec), cheap (<0.01 XLM), and irreversible.
chapter5.lesson3_text: Want to hold a token (e.g., USDC)? You must first set a trustline to the issuer.
chapter5.lesson4_text: Anchors connect Stellar to the real world – they enable e.g. Euro tokens on the blockchain.
chapter5.lesson5_text: Your Secret Key is sacred. No service, no support, no app will ever ask for it.
chapter5.usecases_desc: Stellar is used worldwide – from individuals to financial institutions.
chapter5.usecase1_text: Migrants send money home – in seconds, for cents instead of 5–10% bank fees.
chapter5.usecase2_text: Creators receive tiny payments per stream or article – impossible with traditional systems.
chapter5.usecase3_text: USDC, EURC, and other stablecoins run on Stellar – stable values at blockchain speed.
chapter5.marco_action1: Okay, you know what to do. Send Lena 1 XLM – this is her first Stellar experience!
chapter5.marco_action2: Use your testnet account for the transfer.
chapter5.action_desc: Send Lena her first Stellar Lumens – she's already waiting!
chapter5.action_error: No keypair available – please create a testnet account first.
chapter5.after_action: Perfect! Lena received the payment. You just enabled someone's first crypto experience – that's Stellar!
chapter5.quiz1_q: What is a Public Key on Stellar?
chapter5.quiz1_a1: A password that must be kept secret
chapter5.quiz1_a1_hint: Not quite – that's the function of the Secret Key.
chapter5.quiz1_a2: The public address of your account, like an IBAN
chapter5.quiz1_a2_hint: Exactly! You can share your Public Key freely – it's your receiving address.
chapter5.quiz1_a3_hint: No – XLM is the token, the Public Key is your address.
chapter5.quiz1_explanation: The Public Key is your public address – comparable to an account number or IBAN. You can share it freely. The Secret Key, however, must remain secret.
chapter5.quiz1_hint2: Hint: Think of the classic banking system – what do you share to receive money?
chapter5.quiz2_a1_hint: No – a trustline is not a payment transaction.
chapter5.quiz2_a2: A permission to hold a specific token
chapter5.quiz2_a2_hint: Exactly! Without a trustline, your account cannot receive or hold that token.
chapter5.quiz2_a3_hint: No – a trustline is a one-sided permission from your account for a token.
chapter5.quiz2_explanation: A trustline is an explicit permission you must set for every token (except XLM). Only then can your account hold and receive that token.
chapter5.quiz2_hint2: Hint: 'Trust' means you trust the token issuer.
chapter5.quiz3_q: How long does a Stellar transaction normally take?
chapter5.quiz3_a1_hint: That's Bitcoin speed – Stellar is much faster.
chapter5.quiz3_a2_hint: No – Stellar is much faster than traditional banks.
chapter5.quiz3_a3_hint: Correct! Stellar confirms transactions in about 3–5 seconds – a huge advantage over Bitcoin or banks.
chapter5.quiz3_explanation: Stellar uses the Stellar Consensus Protocol (SCP), which finalizes transactions in 3–5 seconds. This makes it ideal for everyday payments.
chapter5.quiz3_hint2: Hint: Stellar was specifically designed for fast payments – think real-time banking.
chapter5.quiz4_q: What does an Anchor do on Stellar?
chapter5.quiz4_a1: It secures the network like a validator
chapter5.quiz4_a1_hint: No – that's the job of validators in the network.
chapter5.quiz4_a2: It creates new Stellar accounts for users
chapter5.quiz4_a2_hint: No – accounts can be created by anyone, that's not the anchor function.
chapter5.quiz4_a3: It connects fiat currencies (e.g., EUR) to the Stellar blockchain
chapter5.quiz4_a3_hint: Exactly! Anchors are bridges between the real financial world and Stellar – they issue tokens like EURC.
chapter5.quiz4_explanation: An anchor is a regulated financial institution that converts fiat currencies (like EUR or USD) into Stellar tokens. This allows real currencies to be used on the blockchain.
chapter5.quiz4_hint2: Hint: Think of a 'bridge' between traditional banking and blockchain.
chapter5.quiz5_q: Someone contacts you claiming to be from 'Stellar Support'. They need your Secret Key to resolve a 'security issue'. What do you do?
chapter5.quiz5_a1: Send the key, since support staff are trustworthy
chapter5.quiz5_a1_hint: That would be a mistake – no legitimate support asks for the Secret Key.
chapter5.quiz5_a2: Immediately ignore and block the contact – it's a scam
chapter5.quiz5_a2_hint: Correct! There is no 'Stellar Support' that would need your Secret Key. This is a classic phishing attempt.
chapter5.quiz5_a3: Send the key in an encrypted message
chapter5.quiz5_a3_hint: No matter how secure the transmission – you never share your Secret Key.
chapter5.quiz5_explanation: No legitimate service, authority, or support ever asks for your Secret Key. Whoever knows it controls your account completely. Block and report is the only right answer.
chapter5.quiz5_hint2: Hint: Imagine someone asking for your online banking PIN over the phone.
chapter5.learning1: Stellar enables global payments in seconds for cents
chapter5.learning2: Trustlines are the foundation for tokens on Stellar
chapter5.learning3: Anchors connect fiat currencies to the blockchain
chapter5.learning4: Never share your Secret Key – not even with 'support'
chapter5.learning5: Stellar is used worldwide – from remittances to DeFi
chapter5.subtitle: Decentralization & Security

action.redo_keypair_warning: Warning: A new keypair will overwrite the old one. Your existing Testnet account will be lost.

payment.title: Send Payment
payment.memo_hint: Tip: Exchanges often require a Memo for identification
payment.error_secret_key: That is a Secret Key! A Secret Key is never a recipient address. Never enter your Secret Key anywhere.
payment.error_invalid_key: Not a valid Stellar Public Key. Public Keys start with 'G' and are 56 characters long.
payment.error_own_key: That is your own Public Key. You cannot send a payment to yourself.

chapter6.title: The Accountant
chapter6.intro_narrator1: Marco's fruit stall is growing. Dozens of payments arrive on his Stellar account every day – but who paid what and when?
chapter6.intro_narrator2: His accountant Sofia arrives with a pile of payments nobody can trace back – who sent them, and what they were even for.
chapter6.marco_chaos1: I can't keep track anymore! Every day new payments come in, but they all look identical on the blockchain.
chapter6.marco_chaos2: How am I supposed to know which customer made this payment?
chapter6.sofia_frustrated1: The same goes for payroll. It would be great if we could assign those payments more clearly.
chapter6.sofia_frustrated2: I need a reliable way to uniquely identify each payment – without asking every time.
chapter6.lumio_fix: I know exactly how to fix this. Have you heard of Muxed Accounts?
chapter6.explain_desc: A Muxed address (M-address) is not its own account – it shares the G-address but carries an embedded numeric ID. This allows a single business account to uniquely identify hundreds of customers.
chapter6.explain_flow_desc: All deposits arrive at the same G-address – the embedded ID reveals which customer it was.
chapter6.explain_memo_desc: Free text field, not standardized. Can be forgotten or entered incorrectly.
chapter6.explain_muxed_desc: Structured numeric ID directly in the address. Reliable for automated accounting.
chapter6.explain_cta: Got it! To the use case →
chapter6.payroll_step1: The company has ONE Stellar account – cheaper and easier to manage.
chapter6.payroll_step2: Each employee is assigned a unique Muxed ID by the company.
chapter6.payroll_step3: Every month, the payroll software sends the salary to the respective M-address.
chapter6.payroll_step4: Sofia's accounting software reads the Muxed ID and automatically posts to the correct employee.
chapter6.payroll_step5: No manual reconciliation, no guessing, no errors.
chapter6.payroll_note: The employee doesn't need to know anything about Muxed Accounts – the company manages that internally.
chapter6.exchange_text: An exchange uses a single Stellar address for all customers. Each customer gets a Muxed ID as their deposit address – no memo needed, no lost deposits.
chapter6.shop_text: Each order gets a unique Muxed ID. The payment is automatically matched to the correct order – no manual post-processing.
chapter6.usecases_cta: Got it! Now it's my turn →
chapter6.decision_question: How should Marco organize his payroll accounting?
chapter6.decision_a1: Create a separate Stellar account for each employee
chapter6.decision_a2: Use the memo field with employee names
chapter6.decision_a3: Muxed Accounts with a unique ID per employee
chapter6.wrong_a1_text: Separate accounts mean more fees, more secret keys to manage, and a higher security risk. This doesn't scale well for a growing business.
chapter6.wrong_a1_hint2: Hint: Fewer accounts = less complexity. Is there a solution that works with a single address?
chapter6.wrong_a2_text: Memos are not standardized and can be forgotten or entered incorrectly. Automated accounting needs a structured, reliable method.
chapter6.wrong_a2_hint2: Hint: Which method encodes the ID directly into the address – without an extra text field?
chapter6.correct_sofia: Finally! That's exactly what I needed. With Muxed Accounts I can fully automate payroll accounting!
chapter6.action_desc: Send 2,564.88 XLM with memo 'Gehalt April 2026' to the Muxed address (base G-address + ID 42). The payment arrives at the G-address – the Muxed ID is encoded internally.
chapter6.action_success: In the explorer only the base G-address is visible – no M-address. The memo 'Salary April 2026' and Muxed ID 42 are internal features for Sofia's accounting system.
chapter6.action_error: No keypair available – please create a testnet account first.
chapter6.lumio_after_action: See? In the explorer only the G-address appears – the M-address doesn't exist separately on the blockchain. The Muxed ID 42 is an internal feature for Sofia's accounting system.
chapter6.quiz1_q: What is a Muxed Account on Stellar?
chapter6.quiz1_a1: A separate wallet with its own balance
chapter6.quiz1_a1_hint: No – a Muxed Account is not a standalone wallet.
chapter6.quiz1_a2: A virtual sub-address with numeric ID, bound to a base G-address
chapter6.quiz1_a2_hint: Exactly! The M-address points to the same account – just with an additional ID for distinction.
chapter6.quiz1_a3: A special token account for companies
chapter6.quiz1_a3_hint: No – Muxed Accounts work with XLM and all tokens.
chapter6.quiz1_explanation: A Muxed Account is not its own wallet – it shares the balance, trustlines, and signers with the base G-account. The M-address only contains an additional numeric ID.
chapter6.quiz1_hint2: Hint: Think of a bank account number with an embedded reference number – one account, many IDs.
chapter6.quiz2_q: Why are Muxed Accounts better than memos for payroll accounting?
chapter6.quiz2_a1: Muxed Accounts are cheaper than memos
chapter6.quiz2_a1_hint: No – transaction fees are the same.
chapter6.quiz2_a2: Memos are prohibited for business payments
chapter6.quiz2_a2_hint: No – memos are allowed, just not ideal for automation.
chapter6.quiz2_a3: Muxed IDs are structured and reliable; memos can be missing or wrong
chapter6.quiz2_a3_hint: Exactly! The ID is encoded directly in the address – it can't be forgotten or entered incorrectly.
chapter6.quiz2_explanation: Memos are free text fields and not standardized. They can be forgotten, entered incorrectly, or intentionally omitted. Muxed IDs are part of the address itself – structured and reliably machine-readable.
chapter6.quiz2_hint2: Hint: What happens if an employee simply leaves the memo field empty when making a transfer?
chapter6.quiz3_q: Does an employee need their own Stellar wallet to receive a Muxed payment?
chapter6.quiz3_a1: No – the employee has their own wallet; the Muxed ID is managed internally by the company
chapter6.quiz3_a1_hint: Correct! The Muxed ID is an internal accounting feature – the employee receives on their own regular address.
chapter6.quiz3_a2: Yes – each employee needs an M-address as their own wallet
chapter6.quiz3_a2_hint: No – M-addresses are not standalone wallets.
chapter6.quiz3_a3: No – employees don't need a Stellar account at all
chapter6.quiz3_a3_hint: Not quite – employees can have their own Stellar accounts; the Muxed ID is only relevant for the accounting side.
chapter6.quiz3_explanation: Muxed IDs are an internal organizational tool of the company. The employee has their own Stellar account. The M-address helps Sofia's software internally assign incoming payments.
chapter6.quiz3_hint2: Hint: Muxed Accounts are designed from the company's perspective – not the recipient's.
chapter6.learning1: A Muxed Account is a virtual sub-address (M-address) on a Stellar base address
chapter6.learning2: Muxed Accounts simplify payroll: one address, many IDs – fully automatable
chapter6.learning3: Crypto exchanges and online shops use Muxed Accounts for unique customer assignment
chapter6.learning4: Memos can be forgotten – Muxed IDs are encoded in the address and reliable
chapter6.subtitle: Muxed Accounts
chapter6.narrator_before_action: So the employee knows what the payment is for, the employer enters in the memo field: 'Salary April 2026'. This makes the payment clear – even though the explorer only shows the G-address.
chapter6.lumio_after_action2: The memo 'Salary April 2026' connects both worlds: the employee instantly sees what the payment is for – and Sofia's software automatically credits the right person.
chapter6.action_customer_label: Send 150 XLM as a customer deposit
chapter6.action_customer_desc: Simulate a customer deposit: 150 XLM to Marco's Muxed address (ID 42). The payment arrives at Marco's G-account – Sofia's software reads ID 42 and instantly knows: this is Lena's order.
chapter6.lumio_after_example1: See? In the explorer only the G-address appears – no hint of Lena or ID 42. The Muxed ID is an internal marker. Sofia's accounting system reads it and automatically assigns the payment to Lena.
chapter6.lumio_bridge: This works the other way around too – not just for receiving, but also for sending. Marco pays salaries from a single company account. Each employee has their own Muxed ID.
chapter6.lumio_payroll_intro: Anna, Ben, Mia – every employee gets their own Muxed ID. Marco sends all salaries from one single company account. Sofia's software sorts everything automatically. No more manual follow-up.
chapter6.sofia_payroll_bridge: Marco, the same applies to payroll. Every employee payment looks the same on the blockchain.
chapter6.quiz4_q: Why is a Muxed address better than a memo for receiving customer deposits?
chapter6.quiz4_a1: The Muxed ID is embedded directly in the address – it can't be missing or wrong
chapter6.quiz4_a1_hint: Exactly! The ID is part of the address itself – no manual step needed.
chapter6.quiz4_a2: Muxed addresses are cheaper than regular payments
chapter6.quiz4_a2_hint: No – transaction fees are the same.
chapter6.quiz4_a3: Each Muxed ID creates a new Stellar account for the customer
chapter6.quiz4_a3_hint: No – no new account is created.
chapter6.quiz4_explanation: A memo must be entered manually and can be missing or wrong. A Muxed ID is embedded directly in the receiving address – it's always there and machine-readable without any extra step.
chapter6.quiz4_hint2: Hint: What happens if a customer forgets to fill in the memo field?

chapter7.title: The Vault
chapter7.intro_narrator1: Marco now has a business partner: Rosa. Together they run a growing company. But there's a problem...
chapter7.intro_narrator2: When only one person controls the company wallet, things can go wrong – through loss of trust or lost keys.
chapter7.marco_problem1: Rosa, I'm worried. What happens to our money if I lose my secret key?
chapter7.marco_problem2: Or what if you can never really trust me because I control everything alone?
chapter7.rosa_concern: That's exactly what worries me too, Marco. We need a solution where we're both safe.
chapter7.lumio_solution: I know exactly what you need – a vault that only you can open together. That's called Multisignature!
chapter7.explain_desc: Normally a single key controls a wallet. With Multisig you define how many out of how many possible signers must approve a transaction.
chapter7.explain_1of1_desc: One key controls everything. Whoever has this key has full access – no protection against misuse or loss.
chapter7.explain_multisig_desc: Multiple signers, defined threshold. Only when enough signatures are present will the transaction be executed.
chapter7.explain_threshold_desc: The threshold is the minimum number of signatures required to authorize a transaction. With 2-of-3, any 2 of the 3 signers must sign.
chapter7.explain_vault_desc: Imagine a vault with multiple locks – all required keys must be turned simultaneously.
chapter7.family_desc: Elena and David set up a shared savings wallet. Both must sign every transaction – maximum security, zero impulse purchases.
chapter7.family_scenario_text: David wants to buy a new coffee machine. He sends a transaction request – Elena must also sign before the money is deducted. Both have equal control.
chapter7.family_risk: Risk: If one parent loses their secret key, the wallet is permanently locked. 2-of-2 means zero fault tolerance – both signatures are always required.
chapter7.d1_question: Elena has lost her secret key. David still has his. What happens to the 2-of-2 wallet?
chapter7.d1_a1: David can access the funds alone since he still has his key
chapter7.d1_a2: They can reset the wallet with their email address
chapter7.d1_a3: The wallet is permanently inaccessible – both signatures are always required
chapter7.d1_wrong_a1_text: With a 2-of-2 multisig wallet, BOTH signatures are ALWAYS required. Even if David has his key – without Elena's key no transaction is possible. That is the strength and simultaneously the risk.
chapter7.d1_wrong_a1_hint2: Tip: 2-of-2 means the threshold is always 2 – no exceptions, no bypassing.
chapter7.d1_wrong_a2_title: Wrong – blockchain has no password reset
chapter7.d1_wrong_a2_text: The blockchain has no email recovery, no support and no password reset. The key IS the access – without it there is no way to access the wallet.
chapter7.d1_wrong_a2_hint2: Tip: On the blockchain there is no central authority that could reset keys – that is intentional, but comes with responsibility.
chapter7.d1_correct_lumio: Exactly! That is the fundamental risk of 2-of-2: if one key is lost, the funds are forever unreachable. That's why secure storage is so important.
chapter7.company_desc: Marco, Rosa and lawyer Dr. Weber are three signers. Any two must sign – security with fault tolerance.
chapter7.company_scenario_text: Marco wants to pay a supplier → Rosa co-signs → done. Dr. Weber is not needed. If Marco is on vacation, Rosa and Dr. Weber can act together. The company stays operational.
chapter7.company_risk: 2-of-3 balances security with fault tolerance: even if one signer loses their key, the other two can still act. Only when two keys are lost does the threshold become unreachable.
chapter7.d2_question: Marco, Rosa and Dr. Weber run a company with 3 partners. Which multisig setup is correct: majority required, still works if one partner is unreachable?
chapter7.d2_a1: 1-of-3 – each individual partner can execute transactions alone
chapter7.d2_a2: 3-of-3 – all three partners must always sign
chapter7.d2_a3: 2-of-3 – any two of the three partners must sign
chapter7.d2_wrong_a1_text: 1-of-3 means that each individual signer could move all funds alone. This offers no protection against fraud, theft or a compromised key – more dangerous than a normal single-key wallet.
chapter7.d2_wrong_a1_hint2: Tip: For company funds the threshold must be high enough that no single person can act alone.
chapter7.d2_wrong_a2_text: 3-of-3 sounds secure, but if one partner is sick, traveling or loses their key, the entire company is blocked. Organizations need fault tolerance.
chapter7.d2_wrong_a2_hint2: Tip: The solution is a threshold that requires a majority but still works when someone is unreachable.
chapter7.d2_correct_lumio: Perfect! 2-of-3 is the most common setup for small organizations – for good reason: majority required, but fault tolerant.
chapter7.usecase1_text: Multiple partners or executives – no single person can move funds alone.
chapter7.usecase2_text: Both partners must agree – protects against impulse purchases and gives equal control.
chapter7.usecase3_text: Decentralized organizations manage shared funds without a central trustee.
chapter7.usecase4_text: You hold 2 keys on separate devices, a trusted partner holds 1 as backup – 2-of-3.
chapter7.usecase5_text: Funds are only released when both parties sign – no fraud possible.
chapter7.quiz1_a1: Exactly 2 of 3 possible signers must always sign, otherwise nothing happens
chapter7.quiz1_a2: Any 2 of the 3 signers must sign a transaction for it to be executed
chapter7.quiz1_a3: Two transactions must be confirmed within 3 minutes
chapter7.quiz1_a1_hint: Almost! Any 2 of 3 can sign – not always the same two.
chapter7.quiz1_a2_hint: Exactly! Any 2 of the 3 signers are enough – that gives flexibility and security.
chapter7.quiz1_a3_hint: That's not right – Multisig has nothing to do with time limits.
chapter7.quiz1_explanation: With 2-of-3 Multisig, any combination of 2 of the 3 signers can approve the transaction. They don't always have to be the same two – that's the difference from 'fixed' signatures.
chapter7.quiz1_hint2: Think of the company vault: Marco+Rosa, Rosa+Weber or Marco+Weber – all three combinations work.
chapter7.quiz2_q: Marco, Rosa and Dr. Weber have a 2-of-3 wallet. Rosa loses her key. Can they still access the funds?
chapter7.quiz2_a1: No – since one signer has lost their key, the wallet is locked
chapter7.quiz2_a2: Yes – Marco and Dr. Weber together reach the threshold of 2
chapter7.quiz2_a3: No – all three signers are always required
chapter7.quiz2_a1_hint: The threshold is 2, not 3 – Marco and Weber can still act.
chapter7.quiz2_a2_hint: Correct! That's exactly the strength of 2-of-3: fault tolerance when one key is lost.
chapter7.quiz2_a3_hint: With 2-of-3 only 2 signatures are required, not all 3.
chapter7.quiz2_explanation: With 2-of-3 any 2 signatures are sufficient. If Rosa loses her key, Marco and Dr. Weber can still execute all transactions together. That's the difference from 2-of-2.
chapter7.quiz2_hint2: Remember: 2-of-3 = one lost signature = still operational. 2-of-2 = one lost signature = permanently locked.
chapter7.quiz3_q: Elena and David have a 2-of-2 wallet. Elena loses her key. What happens?
chapter7.quiz3_a1: The wallet is permanently locked – both signatures are always required
chapter7.quiz3_a2: David can access the funds alone since he still has his key
chapter7.quiz3_a3: They can contact Stellar to recover the key
chapter7.quiz3_a1_hint: Correct! 2-of-2 has zero fault tolerance – both keys are always required.
chapter7.quiz3_a2_hint: With 2-of-2 a single key is not enough – the threshold always remains 2.
chapter7.quiz3_a3_hint: Stellar is decentralized – there is no central authority that can reset keys.
chapter7.quiz3_explanation: With 2-of-2 both signatures are always required. If one key is lost, there is no way to execute a transaction – the wallet is permanently locked. That's why secure key storage is so critical.
chapter7.quiz3_hint2: Remember: 2-of-2 = maximum security, zero fault tolerance. Every key must be stored securely – in multiple physical locations.
chapter7.quiz4_q: Why is 1-of-3 Multisig risky for a company fund?
chapter7.quiz4_a1: Because the setup is too complicated and confuses employees
chapter7.quiz4_a2: Because Stellar only supports 2-of-3 or higher thresholds
chapter7.quiz4_a3: Because each individual signer can move all funds alone – no protection against fraud
chapter7.quiz4_a1_hint: That's not the reason – the threshold makes the setup risky.
chapter7.quiz4_a2_hint: Stellar supports flexible thresholds – that's not the problem.
chapter7.quiz4_a3_hint: Exactly! 1-of-3 means no real majority control – anyone can act alone.
chapter7.quiz4_explanation: With 1-of-3 each of the three signers can move all funds alone. That's worse than a normal single-key wallet, because three different people are a potential attack target.
chapter7.quiz4_hint2: Think of a company fund: you don't want a single employee to be able to initiate transfers alone.
chapter7.cert_tagline: You are now a Stellar pro!
chapter7.learning1: Multisignature requires multiple signatures to execute a transaction
chapter7.learning2: 2-of-3 provides fault tolerance: one lost key does not lock the wallet
chapter7.learning3: Multisig is suitable for companies, families, DAOs and high-security wallets
chapter7.learning4: 2-of-2 has zero fault tolerance – if one key is lost, the funds are forever unreachable
chapter7.subtitle: Multisignature

chapter8.title: The Automatic Contract
chapter8.description: Lumio meets Marco, who never wants to be cheated out of his payment again. Together they discover Soroban – Stellar's smart contract platform.

scene1.dialog1: Lumio, I'm fed up. Last week I had a buyer – I delivered, and then he just didn't pay.
scene1.dialog2: I know the feeling. And if you ask for payment upfront, the buyer doesn't trust you.
scene1.dialog3: Exactly that dilemma! Buyer doesn't trust seller. Seller doesn't trust buyer.
scene1.dialog4: What if neither had to trust the other?
scene1.dialog6: With a contract that executes itself.

scene2.section: Scene 2 – What is a Smart Contract?
scene2.dialog1: Imagine a vending machine. You put in a coin – it gives you a soda. No seller, no negotiation, no exceptions.
scene2.dialog2: Sure, but that's just a machine.
scene2.dialog3: A smart contract is the same – but for money and conditions. 'If Marco delivers AND buyer confirms → then pay automatically.' The code decides, not a person.
scene2.dialog5: Nobody – it runs on the blockchain. Immutable.

scene3.dialog1: Stellar has its own smart contract platform – it's called Soroban.
scene3.dialog2: I've heard of something like that from Ethereum. Is it the same?
scene3.dialog3: Similar concept, but Stellar is faster, cheaper and ready to use for simple tasks.
scene3.dialog5: Imagine you could deposit the money with a neutral trustee – who only releases it when both parties are satisfied. That's exactly what's called escrow.

pre.dialog1: Let's try this right now! I'll show you how in a moment – but first let me explain what escrow actually means.
pre.dialog3: Stellar has built this trustee principle directly into the protocol – as a native feature called Claimable Balance. An amount of XLM or tokens is locked and tied to a condition: who may claim it? From when? And until when can the sender reclaim it? Only when the condition is met can the recipient withdraw the funds.
pre.dialog4: So we're locking testnet money now?

action.label: Create Escrow
action.description: Locks 50 XLM on the testnet for a demo buyer. The buyer can claim them immediately; you can reclaim them after 1 hour if nothing happens.
action.locked_note: The 50 XLM are now locked – neither you nor the buyer can simply withdraw them. The funds are waiting for the condition.

post.dialog1: That was simpler than I thought!
post.dialog2: That is how decentralized trust works. The contract now runs on the blockchain – no middleman, no exceptions.

intro.dialog1: Marco, what you just did – that's only the beginning.
intro.dialog3: Everywhere a middleman, a notary or a bank stands between two people today – a smart contract could stand there tomorrow.

list.item1: Property purchase – The purchase price is released automatically once the land registry entry is confirmed. No notary needed.
list.item2: Notarizations – Contracts stored on-chain, immutable and provable forever. No paperwork.
list.item3: Music licensing – Royalties flow automatically to the artist with every purchase or stream.
list.item4: Insurance – Claim payment is triggered automatically once a condition is met (e.g. flight delay over 3 hours).
list.item5: Voting – Tamper-proof votes for companies, associations or municipalities.
list.item6: Certificates – Diplomas and qualifications stored on-chain. Never lost, never forged.

outro.dialog2: In five years it will be everyday life.
outro.dialog3: And all of it without a bank?
outro.dialog4: Without a bank. Without waiting. Without fees for the middleman.

scene6.dialog1: Sounds perfect. Are there no weaknesses at all?
scene6.dialog2: There are – but they're not with Soroban itself.
scene6.dialog3: Soroban does exactly what the developer writes into it. Nothing more, nothing less.
scene6.dialog4: The real risk: a poorly written contract has gaps – and on the blockchain there's no 'undo'.
scene6.dialog5: So it's about the craftsman, not the tool?
scene6.dialog6: Exactly. Soroban is the tool. Whether it works well depends on the craftsman.

choice.question: What is the biggest risk with a smart contract?
choice.a2: The developer doesn't know Soroban well enough
choice.a3: Smart contracts are too slow for Stellar
choice.correct: Correct! Soroban is the tool – the developer determines the quality.
choice.wrong: Not quite. Soroban is technically mature for simple applications. The real risk lies with the developer – they need to know Soroban well to write secure contracts.
choice.hint2: Hint: Lumio said 'Soroban is the tool. Whether it works well depends on the...'

quiz.q1: What does a smart contract do automatically?
quiz.q1_a1: It executes an action as soon as a condition is met.
quiz.q1_a2: It exchanges cryptocurrency into fiat money.
quiz.q1_a3: It protects your wallet from hackers.
quiz.q1_a4: It always sends XLM to Lumio.
quiz.q1_explanation: A smart contract automatically executes an action when a pre-defined condition is met – without any humans in between.
quiz.q1_hint2: Think of the vending machine: you put in a coin and it gives you...
quiz.q2: What mainly distinguishes Soroban from Ethereum smart contracts?
quiz.q2_a2: Soroban runs on the Bitcoin blockchain.
quiz.q2_explanation: Soroban is Stellar's smart contract platform and is faster and cheaper compared to Ethereum.
quiz.q2_hint2: Look back at the comparison card: Ethereum vs. Stellar/Soroban.
quiz.q3: When are the tokens in Marco's escrow released?
quiz.q3_a1: As soon as the buyer confirms receipt.
quiz.q3_a4: As soon as Marco logs in.
quiz.q3_explanation: In the real escrow scenario: the buyer confirms receipt of the goods – only then does the smart contract release the money.
quiz.q3_hint2: Think about Marco's problem: he wanted to be sure he pays when the goods arrive.

summary.learning1: What a smart contract is and how it executes automatically
summary.learning2: What Soroban is – Stellar's own smart contract platform
summary.learning3: How an escrow contract protects buyers and sellers
summary.learning4: What future use cases smart contracts enable (property, notary, music, insurance, voting, certificates)
summary.learning5: That the risk lies with the developer, not with Soroban itself

chapter8.subtitle: Escrow & Smart Contracts – built-in vs. programmable

escrowVsSmartContract.marco: Exactly – and the key point: Claimable Balance is not a smart contract. It is a native protocol feature that Stellar ships with out of the box. No Rust code, no WASM, no programming. Escrow, airdrops, conditional payments – all of that works with built-in features. You only need Soroban when the protocol itself cannot do something. Then you write your own code and deploy it. Two clearly separate tools.

sorobanExample.intro: And now let me show you a real example – a smart contract that actually lives on the blockchain.
sorobanExample.marco: This is the 'Hello World Contract' – the simplest smart contract there is, deployed by Stellar's own development team on the testnet. It doesn't do anything spectacular, but it's publicly on the blockchain and its source code is published on GitHub.
sorobanExample.message: Serious projects publish their source code. You can use it to verify that the code on the blockchain actually matches what's on GitHub.
sorobanExample.fallback_note: Contract not found? Testnet contracts are reset regularly.

chapter8.escrowExplanation: An escrow is like a safe held by a neutral party. The money is deposited and only released when a condition is met – for example, when the work is completed. Neither party can simply access it.

contractVerification.lumio: But how do I know the contract actually does what it promises?
contractVerification.marco: Great question! Soroban contracts are compiled to WASM – machine code. Experts can check the WASM hash and compare it to the published source code. It's like a fingerprint for the contract.

codeTransparency.lumio: But wait – you said 'immutable, visible to everyone'. Who can actually read the code?
codeTransparency.marco: Technically no one without prior knowledge: on the blockchain the code lives as a WASM file – compiled machine code that almost no one can read directly. That's why serious projects publish their original Rust source code on GitHub. In the Stellar Lab and Explorer you can spot verified contracts by a green badge next to the contract ID – often labelled "Build Verified". This means: the developer submitted a GitHub attestation and the WASM hash on-chain was matched against the published source code. If you don't see that badge, you only see the code's fingerprint – but not what it actually does.

oracleProblem.lumio: And if the buyer doesn't confirm that they received the house – how is the smart contract supposed to know? It needs an [[Oracle|oracle]], right?
oracleProblem.marco: That's called the oracle problem! Smart contracts can't fetch external data. You need a middleman – a so-called [[Oracle|oracle]] – that brings real-world data into the blockchain. For example, a notary oracle that confirms the transfer of ownership.

housePurchase.lumio1: Imagine: the buyer deposits the agreed purchase price into a smart contract. The seller receives the money as soon as the notary confirms the handover.
housePurchase.marco1: Perfect example! The smart contract holds the money until the condition is met. No one can simply take it. Only the contract decides.
housePurchase.lumio2: But then the oracle problem appears again – who tells the contract that the notary actually signed?
housePurchase.marco2: Exactly! You need a trusted oracle. That's the crux of smart contracts for real-world assets. Ethereum networks use solutions like Chainlink – it works, but makes it more complex.
housePurchase.lumio3: And is that even legally recognized? Can I buy my house via smart contract?
housePurchase.marco3: Not really yet. In most countries, courts don't recognize smart contracts as legally binding agreements. The technology is there – but the law still needs to catch up.
housePurchase.transition: Smart contracts are particularly well suited for more complex agreements – for example a house purchase where many conditions must be met at the same time.

futureVision.marco1: When land registries, banks, and authorities connect their systems directly to the blockchain – and this is already happening in some countries – no oracle is needed for that data anymore. The contract reads the land registry entry directly. Then theory becomes reality: buying a house without a notary, without waiting, without manual checks. We're not quite there yet – but the direction is clear.
futureVision.lumio: That means what we're learning here isn't science fiction – it's happening right now.
futureVision.marco2: Exactly. Two real-world examples: Since 2012, Estonia records every change to government registers – land registry, wills, commercial register, court files – cryptographically. The data itself isn't stored in the blockchain, but any manipulation is immediately detected. Not as an experiment – in production. Switzerland goes even further: the SIX Digital Exchange (SDX), regulated by FINMA, trades and custodies tokenized securities on blockchain. The Swiss National Bank already settles transactions in digital central bank money. Over 1 billion francs have already been processed this way.
futureVision.note: Estonia uses blockchain for integrity verification: every change to a register is recorded cryptographically and is verifiable – manipulation is detected. A private blockchain (KSI by Guardtime), not a public one like Stellar – but the principle is the same.

chapter9.title: The Power of Recall
chapter9.description: Lumio meets Sofia, who lost her tokens to fraud – and Erik, who warns against too much control. What is clawback really: protection or power?

scene1.dialog1: Lumio – I need your help. I was robbed.
scene1.dialog3: Someone pretended to be support. They said my account had a problem. I sent my tokens to their address – and they disappeared.
scene1.dialog4: I know this. A classic social engineering attack.
scene1.dialog5: I heard there's something like a recall. Can I get my tokens back?
scene1.dialog6: It depends. Sit down – we need to explain this carefully.

scene2.dialog1: Clawback is a feature that an issuer – the one who creates a token – can activate when creating it.
scene2.dialog2: And then they can recall tokens?
scene2.dialog3: Exactly. If clawback is activated, the issuer can reclaim tokens from any address – including the scammer's.
scene2.dialog4: That's perfect! So I can get my tokens back?
scene2.dialog5: Only if the issuer of your asset activated clawback – and if they're willing to use it for you.
scene2.dialog7: Then there's no way back. On the blockchain there's no 'undo'.

scene3.dialog1: Excuse me – I couldn't help but overhear. Clawback is not a solution. It's a Trojan horse.
scene3.dialog2: What? It could bring my tokens back!
scene3.dialog3: And tomorrow the same issuer can take tokens from you – just because they want to.
scene3.dialog4: Erik has a point, Sofia. Clawback is a double-edged sword.
scene3.dialog5: But without clawback, fraud victims are completely defenceless!
scene3.dialog6: That's why I always check: does this asset have clawback enabled? If yes, I trust the issuer – or I don't buy it.
scene3.dialog7: That's exactly the key question: do you trust the issuer?

pre.dialog1: I'll show you now how clawback really works – live on the testnet. We'll create our own token as the issuer.
pre.dialog2: Really live? On the actual blockchain?
pre.dialog3: Yes – on the testnet. We'll activate clawback during creation and send 100 TRUST tokens to a demo address.
pre.dialog4: Interesting. This shows how easily an issuer gains this power.

action1.label: Create & send TRUST token
action1.description: Activates clawback on your account, creates a TRUST token, and sends 100 TRUST to a demo address.
action1.success: 100 TRUST successfully issued – clawback is active.

bridge.dialog1: The token exists. 100 TRUST are at the demo address – with clawback permission.
bridge.dialog2: And now you can just take them back?
bridge.dialog3: That's exactly what we're doing now.

pre.dialog1: You're really doing it? The recall – live?
pre.dialog2: Yes. As issuer we have the power – and now we use it.
pre.dialog3: If the scammer who tricked me had had an issuer like that...

action2.label: Execute clawback
action2.description: Recalls 100 TRUST tokens from the demo address. Only possible as issuer with clawback permission.

post.dialog1: Done. The 100 TRUST are back – the recall was successful.
post.dialog3: That's clawback. Total, immediate, irreversible – for the recipient.
post.dialog4: That's why: trusting the issuer is not optional – it's mandatory.
post.dialog5: I understand now why you warned us, Erik. Clawback can protect – or control.

summary.learning1: What clawback is – an issuer function to recall tokens
summary.learning2: That only the issuer can activate and execute clawback – not the user
summary.learning3: How clawback can help fraud victims – but only if the issuer uses it
summary.learning4: That the same power can be abused for control
summary.learning5: Why you should always check: Does this asset have clawback? Do I trust the issuer?

scene6.section: Scene 6 – Do You Trust the Issuer?
scene6.dialog1: Now that you've both experienced this – let me ask you both something.
scene6.dialog2: Sofia: You've seen how clawback can help. Erik: You've seen how clawback gives power.
scene6.dialog3: And you – what do you take away from this?

choice.question: What is the most important condition for clawback to work as protection?
choice.a: The user must be able to activate clawback themselves
choice.b: The asset must be based on Stellar
choice.c: The issuer must have clawback activated AND be willing to use it
choice.d: The transaction must be less than 1 hour old
choice.wrong: Not quite. Clawback is entirely in the hands of the issuer. Only they can activate and execute it – the user has no direct control over it.
choice.hint2: Hint: Lumio explained – only the one who issues the token holds the clawback power.
choice.correct: Exactly! Clawback is issuer power – whether it's used for protection or control depends on trust.

quiz.q1: Who can execute clawback on a Stellar asset?
quiz.q1_a1: Only the issuer – the one who issued the token.
quiz.q1_a2: Any user who holds the token.
quiz.q1_a4: The user whose tokens were stolen.
quiz.q1_explanation: Clawback is an issuer-exclusive function. Only the account holder who originally issued the token can recall it.
quiz.q1_hint2: Hint: Who issues the token? That same person has clawback power.
quiz.q2: When is clawback a risk for you as a user?
quiz.q2_a1: When you don't trust the issuer – they could recall your tokens at any time.
quiz.q2_a2: When you have too many tokens.
quiz.q2_a4: When the transaction was too fast.
quiz.q2_explanation: Clawback gives the issuer the power to recall tokens from any address – including yours. That's a risk if you don't trust the issuer.
quiz.q2_hint2: Hint: What happens when someone has power – and doesn't use it well?
quiz.q3: What should you always check before buying an asset?
quiz.q3_a1: Whether clawback is activated – and whether you trust the issuer enough.
quiz.q3_a2: Whether the token name contains only uppercase letters.
quiz.q3_a3: Whether the issuer has a blue verification.
quiz.q3_a4: Whether the token is at least 1 year old.
quiz.q3_explanation: Before buying or trusting a Stellar asset: check if clawback is enabled. If so – only trust assets from issuers you truly trust.
quiz.q3_hint2: Hint: Erik's key message in this chapter was: always check if clawback is active – and whether you trust.

scene8.dialog1: I wish the issuer of my tokens had activated clawback.
scene8.dialog2: And I wish more people would check who they trust – before they buy.
scene8.dialog3: Both are right. That is the lesson.

chapter9.subtitle: Clawback

chapterHint: Tip: For the best learning experience, we recommend starting with Chapter 1.
